---
title: "29: Statistics - Causal Inference"
format:
  html:
    css: homework-styles.css
---

<script src="homework-scripts.js"></script>

# 📚 Նյութը

- [📺 Causal Inference: DAGs, Potential Outcomes, Propensity Scores (ToDo)](), [🎞️ Սլայդեր](Lectures/stat/14_stat.pdf)
- [🛠️📺 Practical (ToDo)]()


---

# 🏡 Տնային


---

## 1) DAGs & Causal Reasoning (Lecture 14)

### 01 Surgery Mortality: Simpson's Paradox & DAGs {data-difficulty="2"}
Two hospitals perform surgeries. Hospital A: 1000 surgeries (100 high-risk, 900 low-risk), 90 deaths total. Hospital B: 1000 surgeries (900 high-risk, 100 low-risk), 210 deaths total.

- a) Compute the **overall** mortality rate for each hospital. Which one looks worse?
- b) Now compute the mortality rate **separately** for high-risk and low-risk patients at each hospital. *(Use: Hospital A has 30 deaths among high-risk (30%) and 60 among low-risk (6.7%); Hospital B has 200 deaths among high-risk (22.2%) and 10 among low-risk (10%).)*. Which hospital is actually better in **both** categories?
- c) Draw the DAG: Patient severity $\to$ Hospital choice, Patient severity $\to$ Mortality, Hospital $\to$ Mortality. Identify the **confounder**. Explain how severity opens a backdoor path between hospital and mortality.
- d) **Propensity score adjustment:** Using the data above, model $P(\text{Hospital B} \mid \text{severity})$ and apply **inverse propensity weighting** (IPW) to estimate the causal effect of hospital choice on mortality. Does the IPW estimate match the stratified analysis from (b)?

### 02 Correlation, Causation, and Alternative Explanations {data-difficulty="1"}
A company observes that customers who see its online ads spend 30% more than customers who don't see ads.

- a) List three **alternative causal explanations** (besides "ads cause more spending"):
    - i) **Confounding:** a common cause of both seeing ads and spending
    - ii) **Reverse causation:** spending behavior causes ad exposure
    - iii) **Selection bias:** the two groups are fundamentally different
- b) Draw a **DAG** for each of the three scenarios in (a).
- c) Design a **randomized A/B test** that would identify the true causal effect of ads on spending. Specify: the randomization unit, treatment vs control conditions, the primary outcome metric, and the required sample size (assume a 5% baseline conversion rate and you want to detect a 2 percentage point lift at 80% power).
- d) The company cannot run an A/B test because some customers must always see ads (contractual obligation). Propose an **observational strategy** using one of: matching, propensity scores, or regression discontinuity. Explain which assumptions are needed.

### 03 Collider Bias Simulation {data-difficulty="2"}

- a) **Simulate** $n = 10{,}000$ individuals: $\text{Talent} \sim N(0, 1)$, $\text{Attractiveness} \sim N(0, 1)$, generated **independently**. Define a "celebrity" as someone for whom $\text{Talent} + \text{Attractiveness} > 2$ (roughly the top 2.3%).
- b) In the **full population**, compute $\mathrm{Cor}(\text{Talent}, \text{Attractiveness})$. Confirm it is approximately 0.
- c) **Among celebrities only**, compute $\mathrm{Cor}(\text{Talent}, \text{Attractiveness})$. Show it is **strongly negative** (around $-0.5$ or more extreme).
- d) Draw the DAG: $\text{Talent} \to \text{Celebrity} \leftarrow \text{Attractiveness}$. "Celebrity" is a **collider**. Explain why conditioning on a collider (restricting to celebrities) induces a spurious negative association between the two causes.
- e) This is called **Berkson's paradox**. Give another real-world example. *(Suggestions: hospital admission bias — hospitalized patients with two diseases show negative correlation; university admissions — among accepted students, academic and athletic ability appear negatively correlated.)*

### 04 Instrumental Variables: Distance to Hospital {data-difficulty="3"}
We want to estimate the causal effect of a complex surgery on patient mortality. However, patients who undergo surgery may be systematically healthier (or sicker) than those who don't — classic **selection bias**. "Distance to the nearest hospital offering the surgery" is proposed as an **instrumental variable**.

- a) State the three IV assumptions: (1) **Relevance** — the instrument predicts treatment, (2) **Exclusion restriction** — the instrument affects the outcome only through treatment, (3) **Independence** — the instrument is independent of unmeasured confounders. For each assumption, explain what it means in this specific context and assess its plausibility.
- b) **Simulate** $n = 5{,}000$ patients: confounder $U \sim N(0, 1)$ (underlying health), distance $Z \sim \mathrm{Uniform}(0, 100)$ km.
$$P(\text{Surgery} = 1 \mid Z, U) = \sigma(-0.03 Z + 0.5 U), \qquad Y = -2 \cdot \text{Surgery} + 3U + \varepsilon$$
where $\sigma$ is the sigmoid and $\varepsilon \sim N(0, 1)$. The **true causal effect** of surgery is $-2$.
- c) Compute the **naive OLS** estimate: regress $Y$ on Surgery (ignoring $U$). Is it biased? In which direction, and why? *(Hint: healthier patients both choose surgery more and have better outcomes, creating positive confounding bias.)*
- d) Apply **two-stage least squares (2SLS)**: (1) regress Surgery on $Z$ to get $\widehat{\text{Surgery}}$; (2) regress $Y$ on $\widehat{\text{Surgery}}$. Compare the 2SLS estimate with the true effect of $-2$. Is it closer than naive OLS?
- e) What happens when the instrument is **weak** (barely predicts surgery)? Reduce the distance coefficient to $-0.005$ and rerun 2SLS. What happens to the IV estimate and its standard error?

### 05 The Causal Quartet {data-difficulty="2"}
Inspired by the "causal quartet" paper: four datasets that have the same correlation, same regression line, but different causal structures.

- a) **Dataset 1: $X \to Y$ (direct cause).** Generate $X \sim N(0, 1)$, $Y = 0.7X + \varepsilon$ with $\varepsilon \sim N(0, 1 - 0.49)$.
- b) **Dataset 2: $Y \to X$ (reverse causation).** Generate $Y \sim N(0, 1)$, $X = 0.7Y + \varepsilon$. Choose $\varepsilon$ variance so $\mathrm{Cor}(X, Y) \approx 0.7$.
- c) **Dataset 3: $X \leftarrow Z \to Y$ (confounder).** Generate $Z \sim N(0, 1)$, $X = 0.6Z + \varepsilon_1$, $Y = 0.6Z + \varepsilon_2$. Adjust noise to get $\mathrm{Cor}(X, Y) \approx 0.7$.
- d) **Dataset 4: $X \to M \to Y$ (mediation).** Generate $X \sim N(0, 1)$, $M = 0.84X + \varepsilon_1$, $Y = 0.84M + \varepsilon_2$. Adjust so $\mathrm{Cor}(X, Y) \approx 0.7$.
- e) For each dataset, compute the regression coefficient of $Y$ on $X$, $R^2$, and the Pearson correlation. Confirm they are **nearly identical**. Now argue: what would happen if we **intervened** $\text{do}(X = 1)$ in each dataset? The causal effect of $X$ on $Y$ is different despite identical statistical summaries!

### 06 Propensity Score Matching {data-difficulty="2"}

- a) **Simulate** an observational study with $n = 1000$: $X_1 \sim N(0, 1)$, $X_2 \sim \mathrm{Bernoulli}(0.5)$. Treatment assignment:
$$P(T = 1 \mid X_1, X_2) = \sigma(0.5 X_1 + X_2).$$
Outcome: $Y = 3T + 2X_1 + X_2 + \varepsilon$ with $\varepsilon \sim N(0, 1)$. The **true ATE** is 3.
- b) Compute the **naive estimate** $\bar{Y}_{\text{treated}} - \bar{Y}_{\text{control}}$. Why is it biased? *(The treated group has higher $X_1$ and $X_2$ on average.)*
- c) Estimate **propensity scores** $\hat{e}(X) = \hat{P}(T = 1 \mid X_1, X_2)$ using logistic regression. Perform **1:1 nearest-neighbor matching** on propensity scores.
- d) Estimate the ATE from the **matched sample**. Is it closer to the true value of 3 than the naive estimate?
- e) **IPW alternative:** Compute the inverse propensity weighted estimator:
$$\widehat{\text{ATE}}_{\text{IPW}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[\frac{T_i Y_i}{\hat{e}(X_i)} - \frac{(1-T_i) Y_i}{1 - \hat{e}(X_i)}\right].$$
Compare with the matching estimate. Which is closer to 3?

### 07 Backdoor Criterion in Practice {data-difficulty="2"}
Consider the following DAG with variables $X, Y, Z_1, Z_2, Z_3$:

- $Z_1 \to X$, $Z_1 \to Y$ (confounder)
- $Z_2 \to Z_1$, $Z_2 \to Y$ (upstream cause)
- $X \to Z_3$, $Z_3 \to Y$ (mediator)
- $X \to Y$ (direct effect)

Answer the following:

- a) **Draw** this DAG. List **all paths** from $X$ to $Y$ (both causal and non-causal).
- b) Identify which paths are **causal** (front-door) and which are **non-causal** (backdoor). A backdoor path has an arrow pointing into $X$.
- c) Apply the **backdoor criterion**: what is the **minimal** set of variables to condition on to block all backdoor paths and identify the causal effect of $X$ on $Y$?
- d) Should we condition on $Z_3$? *(No! $Z_3$ is a **mediator** on the causal path $X \to Z_3 \to Y$. Conditioning on it blocks part of the causal effect.)*
- e) Should we condition on $Z_2$? Is conditioning on $Z_1$ alone sufficient? *(Conditioning on $Z_1$ blocks the backdoor path through $Z_1$. Since $Z_2$ affects $Y$ only through $Z_1$ or directly, conditioning on $Z_1$ is sufficient.)*

### 08 Do-Calculus: Seeing vs Doing {data-difficulty="3"}

- a) Using the DAG $Z \to X \to Y$, $Z \to Y$, explain the difference between $P(Y \mid X)$ (observational) and $P(Y \mid \text{do}(X))$ (interventional). Why are they different when there is a confounder $Z$?
- b) Write the **adjustment formula** (also called the backdoor adjustment):
$$P(Y \mid \text{do}(X = x)) = \sum_z P(Y \mid X = x, Z = z) \cdot P(Z = z).$$
Explain in words: we weight $P(Y \mid X, Z)$ by the **marginal** distribution of $Z$, not the conditional.
- c) **Simulate** with $n = 10{,}000$: $Z \sim \mathrm{Bernoulli}(0.5)$, $X = Z + N(0, 0.5)$, $Y = 2X - 3Z + N(0, 1)$. Compute two estimates:
    - **Naive:** $E[Y \mid X \approx 1]$ (just the conditional mean, without adjusting for $Z$)
    - **Adjusted:** $\sum_z E[Y \mid X \approx 1, Z = z] \cdot P(Z = z)$

    Which one equals the true interventional effect?
- d) The true causal effect is $\frac{\partial}{\partial X} E[Y \mid \text{do}(X)] = 2$ (the coefficient of $X$ in the structural equation). Verify that your adjusted estimate is close to 2, while the naive estimate is biased. Explain the direction of the bias. *(Since $Z$ positively affects both $X$ and $Y$ through a path not through $X$, the naive estimate conflates the direct effect of $X$ with the spurious association through $Z$.)*


# 🎲 38 (01) TODO
- ▶️[ToDo]()
- 🔗[Random link]()
- 🇦🇲🎶[ToDo]()
- 🌐🎶[ToDo]()
- 🤌[Կարգին]()


<a href="http://s01.flagcounter.com/more/1oO"><img src="https://s01.flagcounter.com/count2/1oO/bg_FFFFFF/txt_000000/border_CCCCCC/columns_2/maxflags_10/viewers_0/labels_0/pageviews_1/flags_0/percent_0/" alt="Flag Counter"></a>
